items.title: Hook strength
items.description: High-scoring copy makes the coffee moment visible within the first two lines: commute, office entry, afternoon reset, timed pickup, or slower weekend use. Low-scoring copy opens with generic bean or aroma language and never grounds the need.
examples: High signal: the opening includes a concrete time, motion, or use case.
High signal: the first line frames the drink as a decision aid for a real moment.
Low signal: the copy starts with vague quality praise and no scene.

items.title: Menu guidance quality
items.description: High-scoring copy helps users choose between Americano, latte, flat white, hand brew, seasonal items, hot versus iced, and black versus milk paths. Low-scoring copy only sounds appetizing but never makes the decision easier.
examples: High signal: the copy identifies one or two low-risk first-order routes.
High signal: the copy distinguishes coffee styles in usable language.
Low signal: the copy uses only praise words without order logic.

items.title: Scene, audience, and channel fit
items.description: High-scoring copy maps store advantages to real contexts such as commuting, office coffee runs, neighborhood pickup, or weekend walk traffic. Low-scoring copy uses one flat message for every audience and order channel.
examples: High signal: commute copy discusses speed, route, and pickup timing.
High signal: group-order copy discusses cup count, split preferences, and timing.
Low signal: the same broad pitch is used for every use case.

items.title: Flavor-to-decision translation
items.description: High-scoring copy converts roast, acidity, body, and milk fit into practical guidance such as easier black-coffee entry, softer first cup, or cleaner warm-weather choice. Low-scoring copy stops at flavor jargon.
examples: High signal: medium-dark roast is translated into a fuller, steadier choice.
High signal: bright acidity is framed for users who already like clearer black coffee.
Low signal: tasting-note vocabulary is present without order meaning.

items.title: Trust evidence density
items.description: High-scoring coffee copy uses real store evidence such as bean direction, milk base, add-shot availability, pickup flow, house recommendations, and peak-time notes. Low-scoring copy relies on hype, empty popularity claims, or unverifiable ranking signals.
examples: High signal: operational and product details support the sales angle.
High signal: local convenience is described concretely.
Low signal: the copy leans on everyone-orders-this language without support.

items.title: Offer and add-on clarity
items.description: High-scoring copy explains size, add-shot cost, alt-milk boundaries, syrup options, combo logic, coupon scope, and pickup-versus-delivery differences. Low-scoring copy implies universal discounting or hides the price logic.
examples: High signal: the user can tell what changes the final price.
High signal: member or combo rules are clearly bounded.
Low signal: cheapest-language appears without conditions.

items.title: Local fulfillment completeness
items.description: High-scoring copy reduces logistical friction by clarifying store relevance, timing windows, pickup flow, delivery suitability, and peak-hour expectations. Low-scoring copy ignores the actual fulfillment step.
examples: High signal: the copy tells the user when to order for a smoother pickup.
High signal: the store's local convenience is made legible.
Low signal: no fulfillment or route information appears at all.

items.title: Emotional resonance
items.description: High-scoring copy captures the real emotional need behind the cup: starting the day well, resetting after lunch, walking into a meeting more composed, or giving a weekend more shape. Low-scoring copy only describes the product and not the human reason for wanting it.
examples: High signal: the user can see their own routine in the copy.
High signal: the drink feels tied to a lived pace, not just a flavor profile.
Low signal: the store speaks only from its own perspective.

items.title: Risk control
items.description: High-scoring copy avoids health claims, body claims, absolute rankings, unsupported wait-time claims, and universal-seat promises. Low-scoring copy overreaches on wellness, speed, price, popularity, or availability.
examples: High signal: risky expressions are replaced with taste or scene-based language.
High signal: peak-time expectations are framed conservatively.
Low signal: the copy promises fat loss, no jitter, zero wait, or best in town.

items.title: CTA clarity
items.description: High-scoring CTA asks for useful next-step information such as cup count, timing, hot versus iced, black versus milk, add-shot intent, and pickup route. Low-scoring CTA only says come order or message us without shaping the next move.
examples: High signal: the CTA tells the user exactly what to send back.
High signal: the CTA changes depending on solo, first-time, or group-order context.
Low signal: the CTA is generic and does not collect decision inputs.

items.title: Tone consistency
items.description: High-scoring copy keeps the same grounded tone across hook, body, and CTA. Low-scoring copy starts with lifestyle softness and then suddenly shifts into pushy scarcity or hard promotion, which breaks trust.
examples: High signal: the tone stays composed, clear, and useful throughout.
High signal: persuasion comes from fit and proof, not pressure spikes.
Low signal: the ending jumps into panic language that the opening did not prepare.
